frontPage.title: Cities to fight dengue blind
frontPage.summary: Bangladesh's fight against dengue is severely crippled as health authorities fail to conduct crucial pre-monsoon entomological surveys to identify Aedes mosquito breeding hotspots due to a severe funding crisis, following the expiration of the fourth HPNSP program in June 2024 and the cancellation of its successor. While the government has prepared a field hospital at Dhaka Medical College Hospital and directed private hospitals to reserve at least 10 percent of their beds for free dengue treatment, entomologists warn that curative hospital management is insufficient once infections surge. Experts like Professor Kabirul Bashar and Associate Professor GM Saifur Rahman emphasize that systematic, nationwide surveillance—including baseline, sentinel, and hotspot tracking—is critical since dengue now affects almost every Bangladeshi city. Despite the urgency, DGHS Disease Control Director Prof Halimur Rashid downplayed the surveys' importance, suggesting that 'we have to kill all mosquitoes' and that the curative-focused DGHS should leave surveys to municipal corporations. While the Dhaka South City Corporation (DSCC) completed its own independent survey on May 27, the lack of central, coordinated scientific data from the DGHS or the Institute of Epidemiology, Disease Control and Research (IEDCR) leaves the country's anti-dengue efforts highly disorganized ahead of the peak rainy season.
frontPage.author: Tuhin Shubhra Adhikary and Helemul Alam
frontPage.subheadline: Lack of funding results in no surveys conducted on Aedes mosquitoes
frontPage.jumpDetails: Merged details from Page 2, columns 1 to 4, including government directives for free hospital beds, expert opinions from entomologists Kabirul Bashar and GM Saifur Rahman on nationwide sampling, the structural funding gap from the expired HPNSP program, statements from DGHS Director Prof Halimur Rashid downplaying the surveys, and the status of DSCC's independent survey.

frontPage.title: PM's first visit abroad likely to be KL, then Beijing
frontPage.summary: Prime Minister Tarique Rahman is scheduled to embark on his first official foreign tour to Malaysia on June 21-22, 2026, followed by a three-day visit to China beginning June 23. This selection represents the BNP administration's new foreign policy dictum of 'Bangladesh first,' prioritizing strategic national interests over prior diplomatic doctrines. In Malaysia, key agenda items will address critical issues, including reopening the vital labor market—closed since June 2024 due to recruitment irregularities—which currently hosts around 800,000 Bangladeshi workers. Additionally, discussions will cover the welfare of the 12,000 Bangladeshi students currently in Malaysia, and potential cooperation in semiconductors and halal food. In Beijing, the Prime Minister aims to revitalize Chinese investment, which had slowed due to recent political transition. With China serving as Bangladesh's largest import source at $25 billion annually, the bilateral talks will seek fresh infrastructure funding and support for the pivotal Teesta River management project, establishing a balanced and pragmatic foundation for the new government's external relations.
frontPage.author: Porimol Palma
frontPage.subheadline: Tarique Rahman is likely to make his first overseas visit to Malaysia on June 21-22
frontPage.jumpDetails: Integrated critical details from the Page 2 continuation column, including the BNP's 'Bangladesh first' policy shift, details of previous preparatory ministerial visits, specific statistics regarding the Malaysian labor market (800,000 workers) and student population (12,000), as well as details on China's $25 billion import status, investment lulls, and the Teesta River project agenda.

frontPage.title: Khalilur elected UNGA president
frontPage.summary: Foreign Minister Khalilur Rahman has been elected president of the 81st session of the United Nations General Assembly (UNGA), securing 99 votes against Cyprus's candidate, Ambassador Andreas S. Kakouris, who received 91 votes. Prime Minister Tarique Rahman congratulated him, calling it a major diplomatic achievement reflecting Bangladesh's growing global credibility. In his post-election address, Khalilur pledged to strengthen multilateralism, champion the interests of developing countries, advance the Sustainable Development Goals, and amplify the voice of the Global South, particularly climate-vulnerable states. Bangladesh, which formally initiated its UNGA presidency bid in 2020 and nominated Khalilur in February, last held this prestigious post in 1986-87 under Humayun Rasheed Chowdhury. The Bangladesh Permanent Mission to the UN hailed this landmark victory as a strong testament to the international community's trust in Bangladesh's diplomatic stature and its active, constructive engagement in global peace, security, and sustainable development.
frontPage.author: Staff Correspondent
frontPage.subheadline: Foreign Minister Khalilur Rahman has been elected president of the 81st session of the UN General Assembly
frontPage.jumpDetails: Merged statements from Prime Minister Tarique Rahman's Facebook post, Khalilur's post-election pledges regarding the Global South and climate-vulnerable states, Bangladesh's nomination timeline, historical context of Bangladesh's previous UNGA presidency in 1986-87, and the statement from the Bangladesh Permanent Mission to the UN.

frontPage.title: Testifying ends in a day, arguments today
frontPage.summary: A special tribunal in Dhaka completed the recording of witness testimonies within a single day for the rape and murder case of eight-year-old schoolgirl Ramisa Akter. The fast-tracked proceedings came just a day after charges were framed against the sole accused, Sohel Rana, 31, and his wife, Swapna Khatun, 26, following nationwide outrage over the horrific crime. During the emotive hearing, the victim's father, Abdul Hannan Mollah, testified how he and others broke down the neighbor's door with a hammer on May 19, discovering Ramisa's head concealed inside a bucket beneath a steel bed. The victim's mother, Parveen Akhter, recounted pleading with Swapna to open the door, after which Swapna blamed her husband, Sohel, for the rape and murder before his escape through a window grill. Represented by state defense counsel Musa Kalanullah, the defense cross-examined sixteen prosecution witnesses, which included a minor testifying via camera. Following the submission of the charge sheet by SI Ohiduzzaman on May 24, legal advocates praised the unprecedented speed of the trial, voicing hope that such swift proceedings will restore public faith in the judiciary and be replicated in future rape and abuse cases.
frontPage.author: Staff Correspondent
frontPage.subheadline: A special tribunal in Dhaka completed recording witness testimony in the case filed over the rape and murder of eight-year-old schoolgirl Ramisa Akter.
frontPage.jumpDetails: Merged witness testimonies from Ramisa's parents (Abdul Hannan Mollah and Parveen Akhter), details of the discovery of the victim's body, the defense representation by Musa Kalanullah, and legal reactions to the trial's swiftness from Columns 1 and 2 on Page 2.

frontPage.title: CPJ urges govt to act on press freedom pledge
frontPage.summary: Marking the first 100 days of the BNP government's tenure, the Committee to Protect Journalists (CPJ) has urged Prime Minister Tarique Rahman to actively fulfill his pledge to protect press freedom by ending the systemic cycle of partisan prosecutions. CPJ's Asia Pacific Programme Coordinator, Kunal Majumder, highlighted that successive administrations have historically weaponized criminal laws against journalists perceived to be aligned with previous regimes. To address this, CPJ outlined a 10-step roadmap, which includes immediately releasing detained media figures such as Farzana Rupa, Shakil Ahmed, and Mozammel Babu of Ekattor TV, and dropping politically motivated charges. The urgency of these reforms is underscored by the Editors' Council presenting a list of 282 journalists currently facing politically motivated cases, as well as at least 25 journalists facing controversial genocide and crimes against humanity investigations under the International Crimes Tribunal. Furthermore, CPJ called for swift accountability regarding the December 2025 mob attacks on Prothom Alo and The Daily Star offices, alongside the replacement or major amendment of restrictive legislative frameworks, including the Cyber Protection Act of 2026, the Anti-Terrorism Act, and the Official Secrets Act.
frontPage.author: Staff Correspondent
frontPage.subheadline: The Committee to Protect Journalists asks Prime Minister Tarique Rahman to end the cycle of partisan prosecution of journalists.
frontPage.jumpDetails: Incorporated specific continuation details from Column 5 and 6 of Page 2, including the 100-day mark context, CPJ Coordinator Kunal Majumder's statement, names of detained journalists (Farzana Rupa, Shakil Ahmed, Mozammel Babu), the Editors' Council list of 282 targeted journalists, the 25 journalists under ICT investigation, and specific calls to reform the Cyber Protection Act, 2026, and other restrictive laws.

frontPage.title: UN body backs Bangladesh's perks till 2029
frontPage.summary: The UN Committee for Development Policy (UN CDP) has recommended postponing Bangladesh's Least Developed Country (LDC) graduation to November 24, 2029, extending vital preferential trade benefits for an additional three years to cushion the country's economic transition. This recommendation, following a formal request by the government, will proceed through the UN Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC) to the UN General Assembly (UNGA) for final approval in September. The extension is highly critical, as studies warn Bangladesh could lose up to $17.5 billion in annual export earnings post-graduation, given that the country utilizes 67 percent of all benefits allocated to the 44 LDCs, with 73 percent of its exports being LDC-induced. Experts emphasize that Bangladesh must now actively engage key trading partners like India, China, and the EU to ensure UNGA backing. Furthermore, CDP Chair José Antonio Ocampo and local experts underscore that this grace period must be utilized to implement crucial domestic reforms, including financial sector stabilization, tax revenue mobilization, and economic diversification, to ensure a smooth and sustainable graduation.
frontPage.author: Staff Correspondent
frontPage.subheadline: The UN Committee for Development Policy (UN CDP) has recommended that Bangladesh's LDC graduation be postponed to November 24, 2029.
frontPage.jumpDetails: Merged details from the Page 9 continuation column 'UN body backs Bangladesh's', including the legislative pathway through ECOSOC to the UNGA, specific statistics showing Bangladesh utilizes 67% of all LDC benefits with 73% of exports being LDC-induced, the potential $17.5 billion export loss, and commentary on necessary domestic reforms from RAPID chairman Mohammad Abdur Razzaque, former Tariff Commission member Mostafa Abid Khan, and CDP Chair José Antonio Ocampo.

frontPage.title: 'Don't let it out': Mamata claims Amit Shah wanted to hush Bangladeshi murder suspects' arrest
frontPage.summary: Trinamool Congress chief Mamata Banerjee has alleged that India’s Union Home Minister Amit Shah personally requested her to suppress information regarding the arrest of suspects linked to a high-profile murder of a Bangladeshi Member of Parliament in Kolkata. Speaking at a protest program in Kolkata's Dharmatala area, Banerjee revealed that the West Bengal Police's Special Task Force (STF) successfully arrested a "major killer" and other suspects who had crossed into West Bengal via the Meghalaya border. Following the arrests, Shah allegedly phoned her directly, instructing her to ensure the state police did not leak the development as it "concerned the country." Challenging the Home Minister, Banerjee questioned who ordered the killing and whose names were uncovered, asserting that she remains "a repository of information" even if she chooses to stay silent to avoid throwing Bangladesh into diplomatic and political turmoil. While she declared that "oppression has crossed all limits," forcing her to speak out now, she declined to publicize the specific names of the accused. The Daily Star has emailed Home Minister Amit Shah's office and the Indian High Commission in Dhaka for comments, though a response is still pending.
frontPage.author: Star Report
frontPage.subheadline: Trinamool Congress chief Mamata Banerjee claims India's Home Minister wanted to keep quiet about the arrest of suspects in the high-profile murder of a MP in Bangladesh.
frontPage.jumpDetails: Merged details of the STF arrest via the Meghalaya border, Mamata's specific quotes about Amit Shah's phone call, her refusal to disclose names to prevent turmoil in Bangladesh, and the unanswered requests for comments from Amit Shah's office and the Indian High Commission from the continuation on Page 5 column 1.

pageThree.title: Measles cases rise after Eid lull
pageThree.summary: Following a brief lull during the Eid holiday from May 26 to May 31, measles cases and associated deaths have surged across Bangladesh, with suspected daily cases exceeding 1,000, including 1,016 recorded on June 1. While health officials, including DGHS Additional Director General Prof Zahid Raihan, report that over 1.84 crore children have been vaccinated and general infection rates may be decreasing, experts highlight worrying contradictions in data. Epidemiologist Prof Mahmudur Rahman, chair of the National Verification Committee for Measles and Rubella Elimination, pointed out discrepancies between general surveillance data showing a decline and contrary DGHS statistics, calling for a rigorous analysis of patient age groups. He emphasized the need to determine whether infections are concentrated in unvaccinated eligible children—indicating gaps in vaccine coverage—or in infants under six months who require protective measures like home isolation. Furthermore, Rahman criticized the lack of a coordinated response, noting that because the government has not declared the measles outbreak a public health emergency, there is no expert panel meeting daily to issue local directives as was done during the Covid-19 pandemic.
pageThree.author: Tuhin Shubhra Adhikary
pageThree.subheadline: 6 more die in last 24 hours; suspected cases top 1,000 for fourth straight day
pageThree.jumpDetails: Merged details from the Page 4 continuation (columns 4 and 5), including post-Eid admission numbers, vaccine coverage stats (1.84 crore children), comments from DGHS's Prof Zahid Raihan on declining trends, and an in-depth analysis by epidemiologist Prof Mahmudur Rahman regarding data discrepancies, patient age analysis, and the absence of a declared public health emergency.

pageThree.title: From chaos to compliance
pageThree.summary: The implementation of AI-enabled traffic enforcement cameras in Dhaka is showing promising early results, with traffic officers reporting that nearly 60 percent of vehicles now voluntarily comply with traffic signals, even during early morning hours. Motorists have welcomed the initiative for saving time and reducing potential accidents, though some bus drivers highlight a persistent lack of public awareness and publicity regarding how the new system operates. Running alongside a pilot semi-automated traffic signal project inaugurated on August 30, 2025, the system was initially split between 14 intersections under the Dhaka North City Corporation and eight under the Dhaka South City Corporation. Although procurement complications and re-tendering delayed implementation in the north zone, five additional intersections recently became operational on an experimental basis, bringing the current total of active semi-automated signals to 12. Project expert Professor Moazzem Hossain of BUET stated that while plans exist to expand the system to approximately 120 intersections across the city, regulating informal transport modes like rickshaws and easy-bikes remains a critical challenge to achieving full compliance.
pageThree.author: Shamima Rita and Helemul Alam
pageThree.subheadline: AI enforcement brings early signs of discipline on Dhaka roads
pageThree.jumpDetails: Merged details from the Page 4 continuation segment, including the 60% compliance statistic from traffic police, public feedback from drivers, project launch timeline (August 30, 2025), details of DNCC and DSCC intersection allocations, procurement delays in the north zone, expansion plans to 120 intersections, and expert commentary from BUET Prof. Moazzem Hossain on informal transport challenges.

pageThree.title: Don't forget Rohingyas
pageThree.summary: The United Nations Refugee Agency (UNHCR) has issued an urgent plea to the global community to sustain support for Rohingya refugees and host communities in Bangladesh, warning that ongoing funding shortfalls threaten lives and stability in the camps. The humanitarian situation is described as increasingly dire, disproportionately impacting highly vulnerable demographics such as women, girls, elderly individuals, and people with disabilities. This crisis has been severely compounded by a massive influx of approximately 150,000 new refugees who have fled renewed outbreaks of violence in Myanmar's Rakhine State since the beginning of 2024. In response to these escalating pressures, the government of Bangladesh, the UN, and their humanitarian partners recently launched a joint appeal for $710.5 million. This funding is desperately needed to address the most critical and immediate needs of both the displaced Rohingya population and the local communities hosting them. Without swift international intervention, the compounding pressures of new arrivals and dwindling resources threaten to completely overwhelm local response capacities.
pageThree.author: Diplomatic Correspondent
pageThree.subheadline: UNHCR urges continued global support as the crisis marks nine years since the large-scale forced displacement of Rohingya.
pageThree.jumpDetails: Merged details from the 'Don't forget' continuation column on Page 9 (column 5), which specifies the influx of 150,000 new refugees fleeing Rakhine State since early 2024, identifies the most vulnerable affected groups (women, girls, people with disabilities, and the elderly), and details the recent $710.5 million joint appeal by the UN and the Bangladesh government.

pageThree.title: Bangladesh elected vice-president of 114th ILC
pageThree.summary: Bangladesh has been unanimously elected as vice-president of the 114th International Labour Conference (ILC) in Geneva. Ambassador and Permanent Representative to the UN in Geneva, Nahida Sobhan, assumed the role, marking a significant milestone for Bangladesh on the global stage. This election highlights Bangladesh's leadership and commitment to global labor standards and rights.
pageThree.author: Diplomatic Correspondent
pageThree.subheadline: A diplomatic milestone as Ambassador Nahida Sobhan is elected to lead the International Labour Conference.

pageThree.title: DMP to hold drivers directly accountable
pageThree.summary: The Dhaka Metropolitan Police (DMP) has launched a new policy directing that at least 50 percent of traffic violation cases be filed directly against drivers instead of vehicle owners to enforce better on-road discipline and ensure direct accountability. Under this updated framework, offending drivers will also have demerit points deducted from their licenses, supported by an extensive expansion of the capital's traffic surveillance infrastructure. While approximately 900 cameras currently operate under the DMP alongside several hundred private feeds, plans are underway to install semi-automated traffic signals using artificial intelligence (AI) at 120 critical intersections and establish AI-powered cameras at 500 strategic points across Dhaka. Emphasizing the strictly enforced surveillance, a court recently sentenced a 38-year-old motorcyclist, Lablu Haque, to one month in prison and a Tk 2,000 fine for concealing his number plate with tape to evade AI detection. DMP officials warn that a major drive will soon be initiated against vehicles with broken, obscured, or illegible license plates, warning that tampering with registration visibility to bypass digital enforcement will meet with strict legal consequences.
pageThree.author: Staff Correspondent
pageThree.subheadline: At least 50% traffic cases to be filed against drivers, not owners
pageThree.jumpDetails: Merged details from the continuation on Page 9, column 1, which outlined the DMP's AI surveillance expansion plans (120 AI-enabled intersections and 500 strategic cameras), details on the arrest and sentencing of motorcyclist Lablu Haque for plate tampering, and the warning of an upcoming drive against obscured license plates.

backPage.title: Deaths from border crime not 'border killings'
backPage.summary: Home Minister Salahuddin Ahmed asserted that fatalities arising from border crimes or illegal trespass should not be classified as 'border killings,' arguing that how a nation's border force addresses foreign criminals depends on domestic laws. Speaking ahead of a director general-level meeting between Border Guard Bangladesh (BGB) and India's Border Security Force (BSF), he emphasized that all relevant border concerns would be discussed, while confirming that the BGB is on high alert against any illegal push-ins. Regarding domestic issues, the minister addressed the surge of battery-run autorickshaws in Dhaka, explaining that the government is seeking alternative arrangements outside the metropolitan area to ease traffic congestion without creating sudden unemployment. He also highlighted plans to implement artificial intelligence in traffic management to revolutionize Dhaka's traffic flow and assured that residents of Jungle Salimpur would not be evicted without proper rehabilitation, though law enforcement will remain vigilant against terrorist hideouts. Finally, Salahuddin noted that the government is focusing on curbing crime in Mohammadpur and addressing legal complexities surrounding a case involving the deaths of six newborns at Ad Din Hospital.
backPage.author: Staff Correspondent
backPage.subheadline: Says Salahuddin, hints taking baby rickshaws outside cities
backPage.jumpDetails: Merged details from Page 8 column 5 under the jump line 'Deaths from border crime' which elaborates on the BGB-BSF director general-level meeting, policies on repatriation, the relocation of battery-powered autorickshaws outside the capital, plans for AI-based traffic management, rehabilitation for Jungle Salimpur residents, crime-curbing measures in Mohammadpur, and legal remarks on the Ad Din Hospital newborn deaths case.

backPage.title: Iran studying deal to halt war
backPage.summary: Iran is reviewing a proposed agreement with the US to halt ongoing conflicts, seeking an interim deal while avoiding concessions on its nuclear program, even as US President Donald Trump expresses optimism about finalizing a agreement within the coming week. Amid these diplomatic efforts, General Sarder Mohebi, spokesperson for the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), asserted that Iran's military and operational capabilities have actually increased during the ceasefire period, enhancing their operational knowledge of the enemy. However, geopolitical tensions remain high as Iran has threatened to expand its blockade to the Bab El Mandeb Strait at the mouth of the Red Sea if Israel resumes strikes on Beirut. Despite recent US mediation to avert escalation, Israel continued its strikes in southern Lebanon. A partial ceasefire announced by Lebanon proposes that Israel refrain from targeting Beirut and Hezbollah-controlled suburbs, while the Iran-aligned group ceases attacks on Israel. Meanwhile, Lebanese representatives are seeking to expand this ceasefire in talks in Washington, while Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu faces intense domestic backlash over any agreement to halt strikes ahead of his upcoming election.
backPage.author: Reporter
backPage.subheadline: Seeking interim agreement, avoiding nuke concessions; Trump sees deal 'over the next week'
backPage.jumpDetails: Merged IRGC spokesperson General Sarder Mohebi's statements on increased military capabilities, Iran's threat to blockade the Bab El Mandeb Strait, details on the partial Lebanon ceasefire, and Prime Minister Netanyahu's domestic political challenges from Page 8 column 4.

backPage.title: ISLAMI BANK: 'Wife's default loan is no bar to Khurshid's appointment'
backPage.summary: Bangladesh Bank (BB) clarified that the default loan of Khurshid Alam's wife poses no legal barrier to his appointment as the chairman of Islami Bank Bangladesh PLC, asserting that the appointment conforms to legal provisions since the default loan was linked to a separate corporate entity. Appointed on May 24 shortly after the previous chairman's resignation, Alam is a director of Agrocrop Limited, holding a 16 percent stake in the firm, which has classified (defaulted) loans totaling Tk 2.79 crore out of Tk 3.56 crore borrowed from First Security Islami Bank and Meghna Bank. While the Bank Company Act (Amendment) 2023 debars loan defaulters from serving as directors, central bank officials maintained that an individual is only disqualified if they serve as a guarantor to their spouse's classified loan. However, legal experts like Supreme Court lawyer Ashraful Hadi contend that candidates must declare that no loans taken by themselves or their allied concerns are classified. Meanwhile, intense protests by the 'Islami Bank Conscious Customers' Forum' entered their second day in Motijheel, with demonstrators demanding Alam's resignation, the reinstatement of former Managing Director Omar Faruk Khan, the removal of those tied to financial irregularities, and the recovery of funds siphoned by the S Alam Group.
backPage.author: Star Report
backPage.jumpDetails: Merged continuation details from Page 5, including Agrocrop Limited loan statistics (Tk 2.79 crore classified out of Tk 3.56 crore), legal arguments from lawyer Ashraful Hadi, details on the Bank Company Act (Amendment) 2023, and specific demands from the protesting Islami Bank Conscious Customers' Forum.

backPage.title: UN warns of extreme El Nino heat risk
backPage.summary: The United Nations weather agency has warned of extreme heat risks associated with a strong El Niño pattern, which is projected to persist until November 2026. The UN has urged nations to scale up preparedness measures to mitigate the impacts of extreme temperatures, drought, and agricultural disruptions.
backPage.author: Reporter
backPage.subheadline: Urges preparedness as it is likely to continue until Nov

backPage.title: Homecoming ends on the highway
backPage.summary: A tragic road accident on the Dhaka-Bhanga Expressway in Faridpur claimed the lives of four family members, including a 25-year-old expatriate who had just returned from Malaysia, turning a joyous homecoming into a devastating disaster for their relatives in Baliadanga village. The car carrying the family collided head-on with a truck, becoming tightly lodged underneath it, which created significant challenges for rescue workers, according to Abu Jafar, station officer of the Bhanga Fire Service. Although doctors worked tirelessly through the early morning to save the 25-year-old victim, he succumbed to his injuries and passed away at around 7:30 AM. Shibchar Highway Police Station Officer-in-Charge Md Mizanur Rahman confirmed that the involved vehicles have been seized and legal procedures are currently underway as the local community mourns the sudden loss of four loved ones.
backPage.author: Suzit Kumar Das
backPage.subheadline: 4 of a family killed as car collides with truck
backPage.jumpDetails: Fused additional details from the 'Homecoming' jump on Page 8, including the 7:30 AM time of death for the 25-year-old victim, statements from Bhanga Fire Service's Abu Jafar regarding the rescue challenges, statements from Shibchar Highway Police's Md Mizanur Rahman regarding vehicle seizure, and the identification of Baliadanga as the victims' home village.

backPage.title: Body of 75-yr-old recovered from Mirpur
backPage.summary: Following the recovery of the body of 75-year-old Nurjahan Begum (referred to as Fatima in some police statements) from a flat in Mirpur, Dhaka, shocking details of neglect have emerged from police investigations and local witnesses. When police and neighbors entered the apartment, they discovered the victim's room in an extremely squalid, unhygienic condition, reeking of a foul odor, completely dark, and cluttered with accumulated waste and dirt. While neighbors, led by local mosque committee leader Syed Mohammad Hasan Palash, protested and alleged that the elderly woman had been starved to death and left to decompose for over a week, the Officer-in-Charge (OC) stated that the daughter claimed her mother passed away on Sunday and was fed the day before. An autopsy conducted at Shaheed Suhrawardy Medical College Hospital on Monday corroborated this timeline, with morgue sources revealing the body was relatively fresh, attributing the localized decay to her being bedridden on one side for an extended period. Following the post-mortem, the body was handed over to her family, though her daughter and sons remain unreachable for comment despite repeated attempts by reporters.
backPage.author: Staff Correspondent
backPage.jumpDetails: Merged details from Page 8, including the squalid state of the room described by neighbor Syed Mohammad Hasan Palash, the conflicting timelines between neighbors' starvation/decomposition allegations and the daughter's claims, autopsy results from Shaheed Suhrawardy Medical College Hospital showing localized decay due to being bedridden rather than prolonged decomposition, and the subsequent handover of the body to unreachable family members.

backPage.title: Relief for fans as WC broadcast deal imminent
backPage.summary: Bangladeshi football fans received welcome news as a local media consortium, comprising BTV, T Sports, and Star News, is close to finalizing a broadcast deal for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. According to officials, the agreement is expected to be concluded within days, offering a massive relief to millions of fans despite the pressure of limited preparation time for marketing and commercials. The breakthrough follows a protracted standoff with Singapore-based Springbok Pte Ltd, which had acquired Bangladesh's rights from FIFA for USD 7.211 million (around Tk 88 crore) but reportedly demanded an exorbitant USD 12.30 million (around Tk 151 crore, rising up to Tk 200 crore after taxes) for local broadcast rights. After local broadcasters refused to commit to these high costs, compounded by unfavorable match timings in Bangladesh, Springbok surrendered the rights, allowing local channels to negotiate directly with FIFA at a significantly lower rate. This resolution mirrors similar broadcast uncertainties resolved in India and China, where FIFA recently finalized agreements with Zee Entertainment and China Media Group, respectively, ensuring extensive coverage across the region.
backPage.author: Samsul Arefin Khan
backPage.subheadline: A consortium comprising BTV, T Sports and Star News is set to acquire the broadcast rights for the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
backPage.jumpDetails: Incorporated details from Page 8, Column 1-2, regarding the contract values (such as Springbok's initial USD 7.211 million acquisition and their subsequent USD 12.30 million demand), the direct negotiation with FIFA after Springbok surrendered the rights, and the comparative context of broadcast deals in India (Zee Entertainment) and China (China Media Group).
